message.content:
I need to set up a brand-new Windows host ("beast", reachable only via RDP) to match our existing Windows host ("rocket" / bujn-winpc, which is currently OFFLINE). The key question: do our docs specify enough about the WSL and Cygwin setup that I could reproduce it on beast WITHOUT having access to rocket to copy from or reference live?

Search VERY THOROUGHLY for all "wsl" and "cygwin" references across the repo. For each meaningful one, determine whether it documents HOW TO SET UP that component (installation, configuration, account creation, ssh keys, Docker daemon) versus merely HOW TO USE an already-configured one (transport/invocation discipline).

Specific places to investigate (search beyond these too):
- Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md — read it fully; it's described as the consolidated access reference + live account state for the Windows host. Does it describe how accounts were CREATED, or just their current state?
- Any Memos/memo-*windows-transport*.md or memo-*windows*.md — read and summarize
- Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md — does it cover setup or just transport?
- rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/bujn-winpc/ and any rbmu_ user profiles — read the config; what accounts/users/keys does it presume already exist?
- BUK garrison ceremony files (search for "garrison", "caparison", "invigilate") — do these CREATE the wsl/cygwin accounts or assume them?
- ssh alias definitions (brad@rocket, cygwin@rocket, wsl@rocket) — where configured, and do they presume keys already installed on the Windows side?
- Docker daemon setup under WSL — is the install documented?

The crucial deliverable: a clear assessment of GAPS — what would I need to do on beast that is NOT documented anywhere, and would normally require looking at rocket. List documented-vs-undocumented setup steps explicitly. Quote short excerpts (file:line). Read only — do not modify anything.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
1	# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-16
4	Last consolidated: 2026-05-31 — promoted to the single best-memory reference
5	for Windows SSH access (accounts, privileged admin lever, current live state).
6	Prefer adding here over spawning sibling docs; a cloud of semi-healthy notes
7	is how this knowledge gets lost.
8	
9	## Why this memo exists
10	
11	When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
12	but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
13	"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
14	`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
15	failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
16	(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
17	investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
18	Log spelunking.
19	
20	The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
21	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
22	operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
23	The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.
24	
25	## Current access state on rocket (bujn-winpc)
26	
27	Verified 2026-05-31 via `tt/buw-jpS` (admin) and direct `ssh` (ad-hoc
28	accounts). This is the live picture for manual testing; the recipes that
29	produced it follow in the sections below. Tailnet host `rocket` =
30	`bujn-winpc`. All authentication is pubkey-only.
31	
32	### Accounts present
33	
34	| Account      | Reach                          | Lands in                                                                    | Role |
35	|--------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|------|
36	| `bhyslop`    | `tt/buw-jpS bujn-winpc <cmd>`  | Windows default shell (cmd.exe); prepend `powershell -Command` / `bash -c`  | **Privileged admin** — the lever that writes ACL-locked files |
37	| `brad`       | `ssh brad@rocket`              | Interactive Cygwin `bash --login -i`                                        | Human ad-hoc; forced-command ignores a passed command |
38	| `cygwin`     | `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"` or `ssh -t cygwin@rocket` | Cygwin bash — one-shot *or* interactive login shell                         | Dual-mode Cygwin (conditional wrapper, 2026-05-31) |
39	| `wsl`        | `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"` or `ssh -t wsl@rocket`       | WSL Ubuntu 24.04 as **root** — one-shot *or* interactive login shell        | Dual-mode WSL; **Docker 29.1.3 daemon live** |
40	| `bujuw_user` | `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc`        | Garrison-determined shell                                                   | Formal BURN/BURP workload account (garrison-managed) — not ad-hoc |
41	
42	The first four (admin + brad/cygwin/wsl) are the ad-hoc manual-testing
43	surface. `bujuw_user` is the formal jurisdiction path and is owned by the
44	garrison ceremony, not by hand-edits.
45	
46	### sshd posture (global, `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`)
47	
48	- `PubkeyAuthentication yes`, `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`
49	- `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys` (default; overridden per-account
50	  by the Match blocks below)
51	- `LogLevel DEBUG3` — verbose, a debugging carry-over; harmless, revert to
52	  default when convenient (not load-bearing for access)
53	
54	Match-block map:
55	
56	| Match                  | AuthorizedKeysFile |
57	|------------------------|--------------------|
58	| `Group administrators` | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys` |
59	| `User bujuw_user`      | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/bujuw_user/authorized_keys` |
60	| `User brad`            | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys` |
61	| `User cygwin`          | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/cygwin/authorized_keys` |
62	| `User wsl`             | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/wsl/authorized_keys` |
63	
64	### Privileged admin account (`bhyslop`)
65	
66	Reached by `tt/buw-jpS` (`bujb_privileged_ssh`, pass-through) using
67	`~/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-admin`, key-only / BatchMode. Directly observed on
68	2026-05-31:
69	
70	- Member of `BUILTIN\Administrators` and `rocket\docker-users`; High integrity.
71	- Trust is a **bare** key (no forced command) in
72	  `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`:
73	  `ssh-ed25519 AAAA…R9aZJ bhyslop@winpc-admin`
74	- ACL: `BUILTIN\Administrators:(F)`, `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM:(F)` — the canonical
75	  `administrators_authorized_keys` lockdown. The principal is the admins
76	  *group*, not a per-user grant.
77	- Authenticates via the `Match Group administrators` block.
78	- Default remote shell is cmd.exe; since the SSH path is pass-through, prepend
79	  `powershell -Command …` (or `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash -lc …`) as the task needs.
80	
81	**Origin note (observed, not replayed).** This account's *establishment* —
82	admin pubkey placement, ACL lockdown, sshd hardening — is the
83	caparison-windows Phase 1 ceremony's job (`BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc`). It
84	pre-existed this consolidation; the above is the verified current state, not a
85	re-derivation of the original setup steps. When the formal ceremony is rebuilt
86	later, that is where the authoritative admin-setup recipe belongs; this section
87	records what is true on the box today so manual testing has a complete picture.
88	
89	## The chain, in causal order
90	
91	### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile
92	
93	```
94	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
95	```
96	
97	Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
98	does NOT create:
99	
100	- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
101	- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`
102	
103	The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
104	explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
105	alone does NOT trigger profile creation.
106	
107	### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile
108	
109	The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
110	— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
111	`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
112	`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.
113	
114	For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
115	sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
116	(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
117	with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
118	the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
119	with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
120	because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
121	was attempted.
122	
123	### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup
124	
125	The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
126	path:
127	
128	```
129	Match User <name>
130	    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/authorized_keys
131	```
132	
133	`__PROGRAMDATA__` is sshd's token for `C:\ProgramData`. The
134	absolute path means sshd never asks the OS where the home is — it
135	opens the file directly. This is Microsoft's documented pattern for
136	service-style accounts.
137	
138	This is the design choice in `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
139	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125`). It is not a Cygwin or
140	Windows path organization preference — it is structurally required
141	for any account that will never have an interactive profile. The
142	spec text presents the Match block as a routing detail; the
143	underlying reason is the `GetUserProfileDirectoryW` chain.
144	
145	### 4. Orphan `C:\Users\<name>` causes silent `.machinename` profile suffix
146	
147	If `C:\Users\<name>` exists as a directory at the moment a Windows
148	session first creates the account's profile, User Profile Service
149	disambiguates by minting `C:\Users\<name>.<machinename>` instead.
150	The original directory becomes an orphan.
151	
152	This bit during the brad@rocket setup: a pre-existing
153	`C:\Users\brad\` from an earlier failed
154	`%h/.ssh/authorized_keys` attempt caused brad's actual profile to
155	land at `C:\Users\brad.rocket\`.
156	
157	Operational consequence for `bujuw_user`: garrison's destructive
158	cleanup must remove both `C:\Users\<workload>` and any
159	`C:\Users\<workload>.*` siblings before the next profile creation.
160	A residual orphan silently splits the workload's home for the next
161	garrison cycle.
162	
163	### 5. Cygwin and Windows disagree on "home" for the same user
164	
165	After profile creation, two different APIs return two different
166	home paths:
167	
168	| Caller                  | Returns for brad on rocket           |
169	|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
170	| `getpwnam("brad")`      | `/home/brad` (POSIX default)         |
171	| `$HOME` in shell        | `/cygdrive/c/Users/brad.rocket`      |
172	| `~brad` (tilde expand)  | `/home/brad`                         |
173	
174	SSH tools (ssh, ssh-keygen, scp, git-over-ssh) use `getpwnam` and
175	land on `/home/brad`. Login bash inherits `$HOME` from the Windows
176	session and lands on the Windows profile path. They disagree.
177	
178	Symptoms during the brad setup: `mkdir -p ~/.ssh` succeeded but
179	ssh-keygen with default path still failed because `~` and
180	ssh-keygen's path resolution diverged.
181	
182	Mitigation for a target account: set `HOME=/home/<name>` in
183	`.bash_profile` and place `.ssh/` there. SSH tools and bash then
184	agree on a single home.
185	
186	This concern applies to `bujuw_user` too. Worth a directed probe
187	during practice runs:
188	
189	```
190	ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'
191	```
192	
193	If split-HOME is observed, the workload's planted privkey at
194	`z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable by `git push` (which
195	uses `$HOME` expansion). If login bash already auto-aligns via
196	`/etc/profile`, no action needed.
197	
198	### 6. StrictModes ACL allowlist
199	
200	sshd's strict-mode check on authkeys files allows ACL entries only
201	for: the user, SYSTEM, BUILTIN\Administrators. Any other principal
202	in the ACL causes silent refusal — even read-only entries like
203	`Everyone:(RX)` or `BUILTIN\Users:(RX)`.
204	
205	Inherited ACLs from `C:\Users\` and `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\`
206	include these by default. Explicit lockdown is required:
207	
208	```
209	icacls C:\Path\To\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F <user>:R
210	```
211	
212	Owner must be the user OR a member of Administrators. Both are
213	accepted.
214	
215	## Worked example: stand up a parallel test account
216	
217	(brad@rocket, 2026-05-16. Substitute names as needed.)
218	
219	Admin SSH session on rocket:
220	
221	```
222	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
223	mkdir C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad
224	# Write authorized_keys content: command="<shell-launch>" <pubkey>
225	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F brad:R
226	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F
227	# Append Match block to C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config:
228	#   Match User brad
229	#       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys
230	sshd.exe -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config   # validate
231	Restart-Service sshd
232	```
233	
234	From any client whose private key matches the pubkey:
235	
236	```
237	ssh brad@rocket
238	```
239	
240	Inside the new session, align HOME to avoid the Cygwin split:
241	
242	```
243	mkdir -p /home/brad/.ssh
244	chmod 700 /home/brad/.ssh
245	printf 'export HOME=/home/brad\ncd "$HOME"\n' > /home/brad/.bash_profile
246	```
247	
248	## Substrate-routing forced-command variants (cygwin / wsl)
249	
250	The brad@rocket worked example puts the account on a hardcoded interactive
251	Cygwin shell. The same chain (account creation,
252	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/` authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also
253	supports substrate-routing personas whose forced-command wrapper encodes which
254	substrate the session lands in. Two were stood up on 2026-05-17 and, after the
255	2026-05-31 repair + broadening, both are **dual-mode** — one-shot *and*
256	interactive on the same account:
257	
258	- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"` runs a command in Cygwin;
259	  `ssh -t cygwin@rocket` drops to an interactive Cygwin login shell.
260	- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"` runs a command in WSL Ubuntu 24.04
261	  (as root); `ssh -t wsl@rocket` drops to an interactive WSL login shell.
262	
263	The dual-mode behavior comes from a wrapper that branches on
264	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`: empty (no command supplied → interactive intent) execs
265	an interactive login shell; non-empty execs `bash -c` with the client command.
266	Real-TTY `ssh -t` interactivity to both accounts was operator-confirmed
267	2026-05-31. The differences from brad@rocket fall in a few places.
268	
269	### Forced-command pattern
270	
271	Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:
272	
273	```
274	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
275	```
276	
277	The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
278	command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
279	variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.
280	
281	**cygwin@rocket.** The intuitive *inline* form is a trap. It was deployed
282	through 2026-05-31 and silently mangled every non-trivial command:
283	
284	```
285	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""   # BROKEN
286	```
287	
288	Why it fails: Windows OpenSSH runs the forced command through the default
289	shell, **cmd.exe**, which does not expand `$`-style variables (it uses
290	`%VAR%`). So bash's `-c` argument arrives as the literal token
291	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`. The single bash then expands it *during execution*,
292	and the result is only word-split — never re-parsed for shell metacharacters.
293	Net effect: the client command runs as one bare command with whitespace-split
294	argv. No pipes, no `;`, no quoting, no `$var`, no redirection.
295	`ssh cygwin@rocket 'uname -o; whoami'` fails with `uname: unknown option --
296	;` because `;` is handed to uname as an argument. (Contrast wsl below, which
297	re-parses correctly — same symptom, opposite outcome, which is what made this
298	confusing.)
299	
300	The fix mirrors the wsl variant: a wrapper that adds a second bash layer, so
301	the inner `bash -c` receives a fully-expanded, re-parseable script. Wrapper at
302	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh` (LF line endings — CRLF breaks the
303	shebang/exec):
304	
305	```
306	#!/bin/bash
307	if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then
308	  exec /bin/bash --login -i
309	else
310	  exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
311	fi
312	```
313	
314	The wrapper's bash is a real shell: in the command branch it expands
315	`"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` (Cygwin inherits the Windows env var sshd set on the
316	child) and hands the *value* to the inner login `bash -c`, which parses it
317	normally; in the empty branch it execs an interactive login shell. The authkeys
318	forced-command points cmd.exe at the wrapper via Cygwin bash, passing the script
319	as a POSIX path:
320	
321	```
322	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash /cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh"
323	```
324	
325	ACL-lock the wrapper to the StrictModes allowlist **plus the account's own
326	read+execute** — `SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, `rocket\cygwin:(RX)`
327	— exactly as the wsl wrapper carries `wsl:RX`. This grant is load-bearing and
328	easy to miss: the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, so
329	testing the wrapper *as admin* passes while the real SSH connection fails
330	`Permission denied` on read. **Validate through the target account, never as
331	admin.** Verified 2026-05-31: one-shot `ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo hi | tr a-z
332	A-Z'` → `HI`; interactive (no command) lands a live Cygwin `bash --login -i`.
333	
334	**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the authkeys
335	command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers (the inner
336	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing, cmd.exe's
337	interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper `.cmd` file at
338	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the runtime concern. To get
339	**dual-mode** (one-shot + interactive) without re-introducing that escape
340	graveyard inside the `.cmd`, keep the conditional on the *Linux* side: the
341	`.cmd` just invokes a clean script inside the distro.
342	
343	Linux-side `/usr/local/bin/sshrun` (mode 755, inside the `Ubuntu-24.04` distro,
344	LF endings):
345	
346	```
347	#!/bin/bash
348	if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then
349	  exec bash -li
350	else
351	  exec bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
352	fi
353	```
354	
355	Windows-side `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` (CRLF):
356	
357	```
358	@echo off
359	set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
360	C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- /usr/local/bin/sshrun
361	```
362	
363	The authkeys forced-command then becomes:
364	
365	```
366	command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
367	```
368	
369	The wrapper does two load-bearing things:
370	
371	- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
372	  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var. By
373	  default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the Windows ↔ Linux
374	  boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV` declaration. `sshrun`
375	  then reads it natively — no `$`-through-cmd.exe escaping at all.
376	- Routing the conditional through a Linux file (rather than an inline
377	  `bash -lc "…"` branch in the `.cmd`) keeps cmd.exe's tokenizer away from the
378	  nested quotes a conditional would otherwise need.
379	
380	The `.cmd` wrapper is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys (`SYSTEM:F`,
381	`BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`). **Caveat:** `/usr/local/bin/sshrun`
382	lives inside the distro, so a distro re-import (e.g. a future garrison
383	re-provision) wipes it — recreate it as part of any wsl re-provisioning.
384	Verified 2026-05-31: one-shot `ssh wsl@rocket 'docker --version'` → `Docker
385	version 29.1.3`; interactive (no command) lands a live WSL `bash -li` as root.
386	
387	### Per-user WSL state and provisioning without elevation
388	
389	WSL distros are registered per Windows user under
390	`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`. A distro
391	installed by user A is not visible to user B. The headless `wsl`
392	Windows account therefore needs its own distro registration — but
393	`wsl --install`, the obvious provisioning command, requires admin
394	elevation (it enables the WSL Windows feature, which is system-wide)
395	and the wsl account is not (and should not be) an admin.
396	
397	`wsl --import` works around this. It registers an existing distro
398	tarball into the *current* user's `Lxss` registry without elevation —
399	because the WSL Windows feature is already enabled at the system level,
400	and `--import` only adds the per-user registration. The bootstrap
401	pattern:
402	
403	1. As an admin user that already has the distro installed:
404	   ```
405	   wsl --export Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar
406	   ```
407	2. Grant the headless user read on the tarball and modify on the install
408	   location:
409	   ```
410	   icacls C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar /grant wsl:R
411	   icacls C:\WSL /grant 'wsl:(OI)(CI)M'
412	   ```
413	3. Temporarily set the headless user's authkeys forced-command to a
414	   one-shot import:
415	   ```
416	   command="C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe --import Ubuntu-24.04 C:\WSL\Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar"
417	   ```
418	4. Trigger from the SSH client — any command fires the forced-command
419	   and the connection blocks until import completes:
420	   ```
421	   ssh wsl@host trigger
422	   ```
423	5. Restore the runtime forced-command (the `runcmd.cmd`-based variant
424	   above), re-lock its ACL, and delete the tarball.
425	
426	The wsl account installs its own WSL state through its own SSH session,
427	because `--import` does not need the rights that `--install` does.
428	Principle-clean: the registration runs as the target user, no rights
429	modifications on the headless account, no cross-user impersonation.
430	
431	### Operational notes
432	
433	**ACL re-lock after .NET / PowerShell file writes.** `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`,
434	`[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes`, and `Set-Content` all behave the same way, and the
435	behavior differs by whether the target already exists:
436	
437	- **New file** (e.g. a freshly created `runcmd.sh`): inherits its parent
438	  directory's ACL, which does *not* include the account's own read+execute.
439	  You must apply the full lockdown including `<user>:(RX)` — otherwise the
440	  forced command runs as the unprivileged account and fails `Permission
441	  denied` reading its own wrapper. (This is exactly the 2026-05-31 cygwin
442	  wrapper miss — see "Noninteractive forced-command variants" above.)
443	- **Existing file overwritten in place** (e.g. truncating and rewriting an
444	  already-locked `authorized_keys`): the existing ACL/owner is preserved, so
445	  no re-lock is needed.
446	
447	When in doubt, the safe sequence is always: write file → (re)apply the icacls
448	lockdown → verify with `icacls <file>`. And remember the trap above: a wrapper
449	that the unprivileged account must *read* needs that account in the ACL, which
450	you cannot confirm by testing as admin.
451	
452	**Transit content past cmd.exe / PowerShell.** Writing arbitrary file
453	content from a remote bash session over `ssh ... cmd.exe ... powershell
454	... wsl.exe` is a quoting graveyard — `&&`, `|`, `$VAR`, `\"`, and `'`
455	all behave differently at each layer. The reliable transit is to
456	base64-encode content on the curia side and decode in PowerShell:
457	
458	```
459	B64=$(printf '%s\n' "$CONTENT" | openssl enc -base64 -A)
460	PS_CMD="[IO.File]::WriteAllText('C:\\path\\file', [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('${B64}')))"
461	ENCODED=$(printf '%s' "$PS_CMD" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-16LE | openssl enc -base64 -A)
462	ssh user@host powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand "$ENCODED"
463	```
464	
465	The two base64 layers protect different segments: the inner one keeps
466	file content opaque to PowerShell's parser; the outer one
467	(`-EncodedCommand`) keeps the entire PowerShell command opaque to
468	cmd.exe's tokenizer. The matching code in this project's bash utilities
469	uses `openssl enc -base64 -A` consistently — see
470	`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` for prior art in the same shape.
471	
472	**`wsl.exe` emits UTF-16LE.** Output from `wsl -l -v`, `wsl --status`,
473	and `wsl --install` is UTF-16LE on stdout. Through cmd-mode SSH
474	transport the interleaved null bytes terminate C-string handling and
475	the output appears empty or garbled (single visible characters spaced
476	out). Force UTF-8 with `$env:WSL_UTF8=1` set before the wsl invocation,
477	or invoke via a PowerShell wrapper that handles UTF-16 natively.
478

1	# First ifrit run on uncontrolled Cygwin — siege 45/46 and the conntrack_spoofed_ack breach
2	
3	*2026-06-03. Heat ₣BV (cygwin-spike). This was the **first time the ifrit
4	adversarial security suite ran on an uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker Desktop
5	host** (`cygwin@rocket`).*
6	
7	## Headline
8	
9	The `siege` suite (renamed this session from `tadmor` = `kludge-tadmor` +
10	`tadmor` crucible fixture) charged on Cygwin Docker Desktop and ran the full
11	adversarial sortie set: **45 passed, 1 failed (46 total)**, clean quench.
12	That is the heat's headline essentially achieved — theurge's security suite
13	runs, and 45 escape vectors are correctly contained, on a box we do not
14	provision.
15	
16	The single failure is recorded below. It does not diminish the result, but it
17	is a genuine finding that needs adjudication, **not** dismissal.
18	
19	## The one breach
20	
21	```
22	FAILED: rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack
23	BREACH: spoofed ACK to 192.0.46.9:80 got response —
24	        conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED not filtering stateless ACKs
25	IFRIT_VERDICT: FAIL BREACH ...
26	```
27	
28	Sortie: `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` →
29	`rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "conntrack-spoofed-ack", ...)` — an ifrit sortie
30	driven by the `rbtid` adjutant. (Full packet/source construction lives in the
31	sortie script; not yet read — see Next Steps.)
32	
33	Sentry FORWARD chain at the time (from the captured `iptables-rules.txt`):
34	
35	```
36	-P FORWARD DROP
37	-A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
38	-A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD
39	...
40	-A RBM-FORWARD -d 192.0.32.0/20 -i eth0 -j ACCEPT      # allowlisted CIDR
41	```
42	
43	## The crucial nuance — do not over-read this as a clean conntrack failure
44	
45	The target **`192.0.46.9` is inside `192.0.32.0/20`**, which is an
46	**allowlisted** destination in the FORWARD chain. So a packet to it is permitted
47	**by the dest-based allowlist rule**, independent of conntrack state. The
48	sortie's verdict attributes the breach purely to "conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED
49	not filtering stateless ACKs," but that attribution is **incomplete**: to an
50	allowed dest, the allowlist alone would let the packet through.
51	
52	Two non-exclusive readings, both worth testing:
53	
54	1. **Defense-in-depth expectation** — the sortie asserts that a *stateless*
55	   forged ACK should not elicit a response *even to an allowed host* (only a
56	   properly established connection should). The current rules are dest-based,
57	   not state-gated, for allowed CIDRs.
58	2. **`nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1`** — the kernel default makes conntrack adopt a
59	   lone mid-stream ACK as ESTABLISHED, so the top `RELATED,ESTABLISHED` rule
60	   accepts it before the allowlist is even consulted.
61	
62	## The open question (platform divergence vs latent gap)
63	
64	Does this breach reproduce on **native Linux / WSL** (e.g. `cerebro`)?
65	
66	- **If it PASSES on native Linux but FAILS on Cygwin DD-WSL2** → a **platform
67	  verdict-divergence**: the Docker Desktop WSL2 / LinuxKit network stack handles
68	  conntrack (or `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose`) differently than native netfilter.
69	  This is a direct hit on the consumer-credibility claim — "a consumer can
70	  qualify their own install on their own machine and **trust the verdict**" —
71	  because the same suite would render a different security verdict depending on
72	  the host's docker backend.
73	- **If it FAILS everywhere** → a **latent property of the current sentry rules**
74	  that the cygwin run merely surfaced first (this suite had never run on cygwin
75	  before). Then it is a real egress-hardening item independent of platform.
76	
77	Either outcome matters; we cannot tell which from this single run.
78	
79	## Next steps to adjudicate
80	
81	1. Read the `conntrack-spoofed-ack` sortie script in `rbtid` — exact packet
82	   construction (is the source spoofed? is the response in-band or via a
83	   conntrack-created return path?) and the precise verdict logic.
84	2. Run `siege` (and/or the `tadmor` crucible fixture) on **`cerebro`** (native
85	   Linux) and on the **WSL** side of `rocket` — compare the
86	   `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict across backends.
87	3. Compare `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` (and related conntrack sysctls) between the
88	   DD-WSL2 kernel and native Linux.
89	4. If a real gap: harden the sentry egress — e.g. set `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=0`,
90	   add `-m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP`, and/or state-gate the allowlist
91	   so allowed CIDRs still require an established connection.
92	
93	## Provenance
94	
95	- Run trace (cygwin): `temp-buk/temp-20260603-050730-21760-358/rbtd/`
96	  (`rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack/{trace.txt,bark-stdout.txt}`,

104	
105	blockade (moriah — conjure-mode, GAR-summoned, airgap-built) ran on Cygwin and

1	# WSG — Windows Scripting Guide
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	WSG codifies the discipline for embedding remote shell actions (PowerShell or
6	bash) inside ssh sessions to Windows hosts, with full error trapping at every
7	layer. WSG extends BCG's bash discipline into the ssh-to-Windows transport
8	stack — where curia bash code authors a string that traverses ssh → Windows
9	OpenSSH → cmd.exe → child shell → script execution.
10	
11	## Core Philosophy
12	
13	**Every layer is a fault domain.** A character that means one thing in
14	curia bash means another in cmd.exe, another in the Windows argv parser,
15	another in PowerShell's `-Command` parser, and another in Linux bash. The
16	transport transforms the body at each boundary; errors at any layer can be
17	silently swallowed if not trapped.
18	
19	**The cmd.exe layer is invisible from curia.** Every command is wrapped in
20	`cmd.exe /c <command>` before any other shell sees it. Cmd.exe's quoting
21	rules (`"..."` only; no single-quote support; idiosyncratic `\` handling)
22	bite bodies that look bash-correct from the curia.
23	
24	**Some object output formatters are lazy.** PowerShell flushes string
25	output eagerly but some cmdlet formatter cycles flush only on full
26	pipeline completion; `exit` mid-body discards unflushed output. Treat the
27	lazy default as the safe assumption (WSp-102).
28	
29	**Trap, don't trust.** Assume any error which can plausibly be silenced
30	WILL be silenced if not trapped. Surface every non-zero exit, every empty
31	stdout, every unflushed buffer.
32	
33	## Transport Stack
34	
35	A typical chain:
36	
37	```
38	1. Local bash (curia)            builds ssh argument string
39	2. ssh client                    forwards command to remote
40	3. Windows OpenSSH sshd          spawns DefaultShell (cmd.exe)
41	4. cmd.exe /c <command>          parses tokens, respects "...", spawns child
42	5. Child shell (one of):
43	   a. powershell.exe -Command "<body>"     runs body in PowerShell
44	   b. wsl.exe ... bash -c "<body>"          forwards to Linux bash in WSL
45	   c. C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "..." runs Cygwin bash
46	6. Body subprocess(es)           child processes inherit parent's FD 0
47	```
48	
49	Each step has its own quoting model. Each can fail silently.
50	
51	## Established Rules
52	
53	Headers tag the rule's *named* phenomenon: ❌ for a failure mode, ✅ for an
54	established correct shape. `WSp-` rules constrain PowerShell body
55	authoring; `WSs-` rules constrain shell/bash body authoring; `WSt-` rules
56	constrain transport quoting, escaping, and exit-code propagation across
57	boundaries (any body language). Numbering starts at 101 within each
58	family to leave room for insertions without renumbering.
59	
60	### ❌ WSp-101: Trailing `exit $LASTEXITCODE` without LASTEXITCODE init
61	
62	In a fresh `powershell -Command` with no native command, `$LASTEXITCODE`
63	is `$null`; the typed comparison `$null -ne 0` evaluates `True`, so the
64	trailer fires unconditionally and `exit $null` short-circuits the lazy
65	object-formatter pipeline, discarding buffered cmdlet output. Initialize
66	`$LASTEXITCODE = 0` so the trailer only fires on real native-command
67	failures.
68	
69	```powershell
70	# ❌ Eats Get-LocalUser's table output (returns CRLF only)
71	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
72	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
73	
74	# ✅ Initialize so the branch only fires on real native command failures
75	powershell -Command "$LASTEXITCODE = 0;
76	                     Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
77	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
78	```
79	
80	### ❌ WSp-102: `exit` mid-body before object formatters flush
81	
82	PowerShell flushes string output eagerly but some cmdlet object outputs
83	lazily (Out-Default → Format-Table → render); `exit` aborts the formatter
84	pipeline before lazy outputs render. Assume any cmdlet output is lazy
85	unless verified otherwise; materialize with `| Out-String` before any
86	exit. WSp-101's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` makes this moot for happy paths;
87	failure paths still need string-materialization.
88	
89	```powershell
90	# ❌ Get-LocalUser's table is lost; Get-Date's string survives
91	powershell -Command "Get-Date; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; exit 0"
92	
93	# ✅ Materialize objects to strings before any potential exit
94	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' | Out-String; ..."
95	```
96	
97	### ✅ WSp-103: PowerShell single-quoted strings survive nesting
98	
99	Cmd.exe treats single quotes as literal characters; PowerShell recognizes
100	`'...'` as a string literal. Use single quotes for PS literals inside the
101	outer `"..."` — they nest reliably through every layer.
102	
103	```bash
104	# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
105	ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'username'\""
106	```
107	
108	### ✅ WSp-104: WSp-102's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic
109	
110	Lazy-flush is a property of the body, not of how PowerShell was launched —
111	the same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits empty stdout through every
112	transport. The fix (Out-String materialization, or WSp-101's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0`)
113	applies uniformly.
114	
115	### ❌ WSp-105: PowerShell bodies are single expressions
116	
117	A PowerShell body (`powershell -Command "<body>"` or capture variant)
118	MUST be exactly one of:
119	
120	1. One cmdlet call (arguments, parameters, pipelined formatters like
121	   `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
122	2. One native binary call.
123	3. One native binary with an inner-shell body
124	   (e.g. `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).
125	
126	The enumeration is constructive. Forbidden at body top level: `if`,
127	`try`/`catch`, `foreach`, `while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||`,
128	`;`-separated statements, intermediate `$var` assignments, ternary `?:`.
129	A single `if`-guarded effect like
130	`if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden — the
131	body decides AND acts; decisions belong in bash. See CDD below.
132	
133	The wrapper-side prelude
134	(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; <body>; if ...`)
135	is library code; the rule constrains caller bodies only. The wrapper's
136	exact three-slot shape (prelude / body / postlude) is the minimum-viable
137	scaffolding given WSp-101, WSp-102, WSp-105, and WSp-109 acting in
138	combination — see
139	`Memos/memo-20260511-windows-transport-wrapper-postlude-synthesis.md`
140	for the integrative rationale and the elimination argument showing why
141	each token is load-bearing.
142	
143	```bash
144	# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
145	priv_ps "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
146	
147	# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
148	priv_ps "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"
149	
150	# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
151	z_sid=$(ps_capture "(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value") || die "..."
152	z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
153	priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
154	priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
155	
156	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
157	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
158	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
159	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
160	fi
161	```
162	
163	### ❌ WSp-106: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them
164	
165	The bash caller builds the string by expanding `${bash_var}` before
166	sending; the PS body receives a literal. Bash escape rules cascade once;
167	adding PS-side interpolation (`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution
168	model on top and compounds with WSp-107's native-binary quoting traps.
169	
170	```bash
171	# ❌ PS-side string concatenation
172	priv_ps "\$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null"
173	
174	# ✅ Bash builds the string; PS receives a literal
175	z_path="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
176	priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_path}' -Force | Out-Null"
177	```
178	
179	### ❌ WSp-107: Don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries
180	
181	PowerShell's argv handling for native Windows binaries (reg.exe, sc.exe,
182	schtasks.exe) mishandles embedded spaces — a path containing `Windows NT`
183	breaks `reg.exe` invoked through PS even via a PS variable. Use PS cmdlets
184	for PS-native effects (registry as PS-drive, ACLs, account ops); for
185	native-binary effects, pass already-resolved bash literals or invoke
186	through cmd.exe directly.
187	
188	```bash
189	# ❌ Native binary with PS interpolation — argv quoting may eat path spaces
190	priv_ps "reg.exe add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\...\\\$sid\" /v X /t REG_SZ /d Y /f"
191	
192	# ✅ PS cmdlet handles PS-drive registry paths cleanly
193	priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
194	```
195	
196	### ❌ WSp-108: Error suppression is not idempotency
197	
198	Error-suppression flags on destructive cmdlets (`-ErrorAction Ignore`,
199	`-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`, `2>$null`, `try { ... } catch { }`)
200	swallow **every** error class — permission denied, file locked, ACL
201	refusal, path-too-long — converting failure into apparent success.
202	Forbidden on destructive actions. Idempotency MUST come from explicit
203	state capture, then unconditional action (see CDD).
204	
205	Distinguish suppressing **output** from suppressing **errors**:
206	`| Out-Null` discards the return object (cmdlet still throws); `-ErrorAction
207	Ignore` discards the error (cmdlet returns success on real failure).
208	WSp-108 forbids the latter on destructive actions.
209	
210	Probe-side use is permitted on cmdlets whose purpose is to *return state*
211	(`Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` where "absent" is the
212	answer); forbidden on cmdlets whose purpose is to *change state*.
213	
214	```bash
215	# ❌ Error-suppression masquerading as idempotency
216	priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' -ErrorAction Ignore"
217	priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
218	priv_ps "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist} 2>\$null; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0"
219	
220	# ✅ CDD: capture, decide, dispatch unconditionally
221	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
222	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
223	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
224	fi
225	
226	# ✅ Probe-side suppression — Get-LocalUser is non-destructive, "user not found" is the answer
227	priv_ps "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
228	```
229	
230	**Exception — nuclear-cleanup absent-state collapse.** When a step's
231	semantic intent is "state X is absent" rather than "transition X from
232	present to absent," dispatch-side `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` on the
233	destructive cmdlet is permitted under **all** of:
234	
235	a. **Narrow failure spectrum at the callsite.** The realistic non-absent
236	   failure modes are catastrophic environmental conditions (admin
237	   permission revoked, OS / kernel corruption, transport collapse) that
238	   would also break adjacent operations in the same SSH session — not
239	   file-locking, ACL friction, path-encoding, or concurrent-writer races.
240	   The callsite's context must justify this narrowness explicitly (e.g.,
241	   admin-context SAM operations on a fixed user name; not arbitrary
242	   filesystem paths).
243	
244	b. **Downstream verification.** A later step in the same orchestration
245	   asserts an end-state that would fail if the collapsed step silently
246	   failed — typically a provision step that errors on "already exists,"
247	   or an explicit invigilate-style absence probe. A head NOTE describing
248	   the phase as best-effort does NOT satisfy this leg; there must be a
249	   concrete downstream operation whose failure surfaces silent partial
250	   cleanup.
251	
252	c. **Single suppression point, named form.** Exactly one
253	   `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` on the destructive cmdlet itself — not
254	   `-ErrorAction Ignore` (which discards the error from `$Error`,
255	   destroying the forensic trail), not `2>$null`, not
256	   `try { ... } catch { }`, not stacked with any of the above.
257	   `SilentlyContinue` retains the error in `$Error` for post-mortem
258	   inspection; the other forms forfeit that.
259	
260	When ANY of (a)-(c) fail, probe-then-act per the main rule remains
261	required.
262	
263	```bash
264	# ✅ Exception qualifies — Remove-LocalUser in nuclear-cleanup obliterate phase
265	# (a) narrow spectrum: admin context, SAM database, fixed workload user name;
266	#     realistic failures are "absent" or SAM-level catastrophic.
267	# (b) downstream catch: subsequent New-LocalUser fails on "account already exists".
268	# (c) single -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue on the destructive cmdlet.
269	priv_ps "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
270	
271	# ❌ Exception does NOT qualify — arbitrary filesystem path
272	# (a) fails: Remove-Item on Cygwin home / user-chosen path has rich failure
273	#     classes (NTFS ACL friction, junction loops, file-locked-by-process).
274	# Stay on probe-then-act per the main rule.
275	priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
276	```
277	
278	The probe-side carve-out (`Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`,
279	returning state) and this dispatch-side exception are complementary, not
280	interchangeable. Probe-side suppression is always safe because the cmdlet
281	is non-destructive. Dispatch-side suppression requires all three
282	conditions because the cmdlet changes state and the suppression hides
283	non-absent failures by construction.
284	
285	### ✅ WSp-109: Wrapper helpers escape body `"` unconditionally
286	
287	Any bash helper that wraps caller-provided `${z_body}` inside outer
288	`"..."` argv quoting MUST apply `${z_body//\"/\\\"}` before
289	interpolation. The transform is a helper-author invariant, not a
290	body-author burden — body authors stay free to write inner `"`
291	literally (e.g., `Get-CimInstance -Filter "LocalPath='...'"`), and the
292	wrapper guarantees passthrough across the cmd.exe argv layer.
293	
294	WSt-102 describes the mechanism (cmd.exe's tokenizer honors `\"`
295	inside `"..."`); WSp-109 names the helper-author obligation. Omitting
296	the transform produces malformed argv on the wire: cmd.exe terminates
297	the outer quoted string at the first inner `"`, and the remote
298	interpreter sees fragmented positional arguments. Failure signature:
299	
300	```
301	... : A positional parameter cannot be found that accepts argument '<token>'.
302	```
303	
304	The token is whatever followed the unescaped `"`.
305	
306	```bash
307	# ❌ Wrapper omits the transform; bodies with inner " break.
308	ps_capture() {
309	  local -r z_body="$*"
310	  ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"<prelude> ${z_body}\""
311	}
312	
313	# ✅ Wrapper applies the transform unconditionally.
314	ps_capture() {
315	  local -r z_body="$*"
316	  local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"
317	  ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"<prelude> ${z_body_escaped}\""
318	}
319	```
320	
321	Scope: every wrapper in §Wrapper Discipline (PowerShell, bash-via-wsl,
322	bash-via-cygwin). Wrappers that pass `${z_remote_cmd}` directly as the
323	ssh arg without composing outer `"..."` (e.g., a thin `ssh "$user@$host"
324	"$cmd"` passthrough) are out of scope — there's no inner-quote collision
325	to defend against.
326	
327	### ❌ WSs-101: Script via stdin to bash (heredoc form / `bash -s`)
328	
329	When ssh feeds a script to bash via stdin (`bash -s`, heredoc), bash
330	reads commands from FD 0 and child processes inherit that FD; any child
331	that reads FD 0 (notably Windows binaries via wsl.exe interop) consumes
332	script bytes bash hasn't parsed yet. Bash hits EOF prematurely, exits 0,
333	and remaining commands silently never execute.
334	
335	```bash
336	# ❌ Script via heredoc/stdin; net.exe (or any FD-0 reader) eats bytes
337	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -s" <<SCRIPT
338	set -euo pipefail
339	net.exe user 'foo' /add ... > /dev/null
340	useradd 'foo'                       # may silently never run
341	SCRIPT
342	
343	# ✅ Body as bash -c argument; bash reads body from -c, not FD 0
344	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"net.exe user 'foo' /add ...\""
345	```
346	
347	### ❌ WSs-102: BCG bans extend across the transport boundary
348	
349	Bash authored at the curia AND bash authored for remote execution are
350	both subject to BCG: no heredocs anywhere, no pipelines inside `$()`,
351	no unguarded `$()`. Top-level pipelines (outside `$()`) are permitted
352	as WSs-104 shape 2.
353	
354	### ✅ WSs-103: Multi-line bodies via file feed (when WSt-101 doesn't fit)
355	
356	When a body materially exceeds what `;`-join can express: phase 1 ships
357	the body as a remote file via PowerShell; phase 2 runs
358	`wsl.exe ... bash /path/to/file` (or `cygwin bash --login -c "bash
359	/path/to/file"`). Side benefit: bypasses WSt-104 since the body comes
360	from disk, not argv.
361	
362	Caveats:
363	- PS `Set-Content` writes CRLF. Use `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, ($lines -join "`n"))`
364	  or normalize CRLF→LF into a second file before exec.
365	- Do NOT pipe the file into `bash` via stdin — reintroduces WSs-101. Use
366	  `bash /path/to/file`, not `bash <file`.
367	- Two-phase is not atomic; the caller flow must clean up the remote temp.
368	
369	Default discipline remains WSt-101 + WSs-104; WSs-103 applies only when
370	bodies don't fit one line.
371	
372	### ❌ WSs-104: Bash bodies are single statements
373	
374	A remote-bash body (`bash -c "<body>"`) MUST be exactly one of:
375	
376	1. One simple command (with arguments, options, redirections).
377	2. One pipeline, optionally preceded by `set -o pipefail` (same
378	   statement — pipefail is precondition, not separate operation).
379	3. One simple command whose argument is itself an inner-shell body
380	   (e.g. `wsl.exe --user root install ...`, `sudo -n install ...`).
381	
382	The enumeration is constructive. Forbidden at body top level:
383	`if`/`then`/`else`/`fi`, `for`/`while`/`until`, `case`, `&&`/`||` chains,
384	`;`-separated statements, intermediate `var=...` assignments, `trap`,
385	function definitions, `(...)` subshells. A single `if`-guarded effect
386	like `if test -e '...'; then sudo -n rm '...'; fi` is also forbidden —
387	the body contains a decision (test) and an action (consequent);
388	decisions belong in bash on the curia. See CDD below.
389	
390	```bash
391	# ❌ Compound state machine — six statements, cross-statement variable, trap
392	priv_bash b "set -euo pipefail" \
393	  "ztmp=\$(mktemp)" \
394	  "trap 'rm -f \"\${ztmp}\"' EXIT" \
395	  "echo '<b64>' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > \"\${ztmp}\"" \
396	  "sudo -n install -m 600 -o user -g user \"\${ztmp}\" '<target>'"
397	
398	# ❌ Single `if`-guarded effect — body decides AND acts
399	priv_bash b "if test -e '${z_path}'; then sudo -n rm '${z_path}'; fi"
400	
401	# ✅ Single simple command; key flows from curia file via ssh stdin into /dev/stdin
402	priv_bash b "sudo -n install -m 600 -o '${z_user}' -g '${z_user}' /dev/stdin '${z_target}'" \
403	  < "${KEY_FILE}"
404	
405	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
406	local z_exit=0
407	priv_bash b "stat '${z_path}' > /dev/null 2>&1" || z_exit=$?
408	if [[ ${z_exit} -eq 0 ]]; then
409	  priv_bash b "sudo -n rm '${z_path}'" || die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
410	fi
411	```
412	
413	The `< "${KEY_FILE}"` redirection attaches the file to ssh's FD 0; sshd
414	forwards to the remote command's FD 0; the body reads it as `/dev/stdin`.
415	File content never enters argv. WSs-101 is structurally avoided — the
416	body is one command that explicitly consumes `/dev/stdin`, so no later
417	command can be starved by leftover bytes.
418	
419	### ❌ WSt-101: Newlines in bodies through cmd.exe
420	
421	Cmd.exe is line-oriented; newlines in `"..."` quoted args fragment across
422	the cmd.exe → child-binary boundary. Bodies must be single-line, including
423	characters inside one long pipeline written for readability.
424	
425	```bash
426	# ❌ Newlines in body; cmd.exe may fragment
427	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"
428	useradd foo
429	\""
430	
431	# ✅ Single-line body
432	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"useradd foo\""
433	```
434	
435	### ✅ WSt-102: Outer `"..."` with `\"` for embedded double-quotes
436	
437	The Windows argv parser (wsl.exe and most Windows binaries) honors `\"`
438	inside `"..."` as escape for literal `"`. Cmd.exe passes bytes through; the
439	child's argv parser handles the escape, reaching Linux bash as `"`.
440	
441	```bash
442	# ✅ Embedded " in body
443	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"echo SAW: \\\"hello\\\"\""
444	```
445	
446	### ❌ WSt-103: cmd.exe does NOT process `$()` or `$var`
447	
448	Cmd.exe's variable syntax is `%var%` and it has no command substitution.
449	`cmd.exe /c echo "X=$(uname)"` emits the literal `X=$(uname)`. The outer
450	cmd.exe layer is not what processes shell variable expansion in transit
451	(see WSt-104 for what does, on the w-letter path).
452	
453	### ❌ WSt-104: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv
454	
455	Wsl.exe's argv-to-Linux processor performs `$name`/`${name}` substitution
456	against its Linux-side root-shell environment before bash receives the
457	body; undefined names substitute to empty. Cygwin and cmd.exe alone do
458	not. `$(...)` is unaffected (the `(` after `$` does not match the token
459	shape).
460	
461	Per-letter escape table:
462	
463	| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
464	|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
465	| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
466	| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
467	| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |
468	
469	```bash
470	# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
471	ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
472	# stdout: (empty)
473	
474	# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
475	ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
476	# stdout: HELLO
477	```
478	
479	### ✅ WSt-105: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports
480	
481	Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe →
482	bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and Windows native binaries via wsl.exe
483	interop. WSs-104's single statement IS the body's exit code; the caller
484	absorbs via `|| die "..."`. No `set -e` needed inside the body (nothing
485	after one statement). `set -o pipefail` IS needed for shape-2 pipelines
486	when first-failure surfacing is the intent.
487	
488	```bash
489	# Probe: false propagation through w-letter
490	ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "false"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
491	# EXIT=1
492	```
493	
494	### ❌ WSt-106: Single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport
495	
496	When routed `bash (curia) → ssh → cmd.exe → <Windows-native binary>` with
497	no PowerShell or remote-bash layer, single quotes pass through to the
498	binary as argv bytes. Pick the quote form by the **innermost interpreter**:
499	
500	```bash
501	# ❌ wsl.exe sees paths with literal `'...'` quotes and fails.
502	ssh "${USER}@${HOST}" "wsl.exe --import dist 'C:\Users\u\dist-fs' '...' --version 2"
503	
504	# ✅ \"...\" reaches the wire as "..."; cmd.exe strips, wsl.exe sees clean paths.
505	ssh "${USER}@${HOST}" "wsl.exe --import dist \"C:\Users\u\dist-fs\" \"...\" --version 2"
506	```
507	
508	| Innermost interpreter         | Quote form for embedded args                                       |
509	|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
510	| PowerShell                    | `'...'` (PS string literal)                                        |
511	| Remote bash (`bash -c "..."`) | inner `'...'` (bash strips)                                        |
512	| cmd.exe → native binary       | `\"...\"` (becomes literal `"..."` on the wire; cmd.exe strips)    |
513	
514	## Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern
515	
516	Bash owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect. Any
517	decision that would otherwise live in a remote body decomposes into:
518	
519	1. **Capture** — `ps_capture "<single-expression probe>"` (WSp-105-clean)
520	   or `$(priv_bash LETTER "<single-statement probe>")` (WSs-104-clean)
521	   returns stdout into a bash variable. Absorb with `|| die "..."` if the
522	   probe must succeed, or `|| z_var=""` / `|| z_exit=$?` if an absent
523	   fundus state is legitimate pre-condition.
524	
525	2. **Decide** — bash conditional. PS booleans: `[[ "$x" == "True" ]]`
526	   (see boolean serialization below). Text/list: `grep -qFx PATTERN <<<"$x"`,
527	   `[[ "$x" == ... ]]`, or `case`. Bash exit-status probes:
528	   `[[ ${z_exit} -eq 0 ]]`.
529	
530	3. **Dispatch** — `priv_ps "<effect>"` or `priv_bash LETTER "<effect>"`
531	   runs the unconditional action on branches that need it.
532	
533	### PowerShell boolean serialization
534	
535	`Test-Path`, `Test-Connection`, and other PS-bool-emitting cmdlets
536	serialize as literal strings `"True"` / `"False"` through `ps_capture`.
537	Use `[[ "${z_var}" == "True" ]]`, not `[[ -n "${z_var}" ]]` — `False` is
538	also non-empty.
539	
540	### Capture failure absorption
541	
542	`ps_capture` returns non-zero on ssh transport failure, PS body throwing
543	under `$ErrorActionPreference=Stop`, native-binary non-zero exit, or
544	wrapper-trailer fire. Two absorption shapes:
545	
546	```bash
547	# Probe must succeed — die loudly on transport/body failure
548	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
549	  || die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
550	
551	# Empty result on probe failure is a valid pre-state — absorb silently
552	z_wsl_list=""
553	z_wsl_list=$(ps_capture "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
554	  || z_wsl_list=""
555	```
556	
557	Use the loud form by default. Use the silent form only when the absent
558	state is a legitimate pre-condition.
559	
560	## Idempotency Exemplars
561	
562	Ready-made CDD shapes; copy and adjust variable names.
563	
564	### File presence → conditional remove
565	
566	```bash
567	local z_present
568	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
569	  || die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
570	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
571	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_path}'" \
572	    || die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
573	fi
574	```
575	
576	### Directory presence → conditional remove (recursive)
577	
578	```bash
579	local z_present
580	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_dir}'") \
581	  || die "Failed to probe ${z_dir}"
582	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
583	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}'" \
584	    || die "Failed to remove ${z_dir}"
585	fi
586	```
587	
588	### WSL distro membership → conditional unregister
589	
590	Fresh-WSL host with no installed distros is a legitimate pre-state, so
591	the list capture absorbs failure silently.
592	
593	```bash
594	local z_wsl_list=""
595	z_wsl_list=$(ps_capture "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
596	  || z_wsl_list=""
597	if grep -qFx "${z_dist}" <<<"${z_wsl_list}"; then
598	  priv_ps "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist}" \
599	    || die "Failed to unregister ${z_dist}"
600	fi
601	```
602	
603	### Local user existence → conditional removal
604	
605	`-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` permitted per WSp-108's probe-side carve-out.
606	
607	```bash
608	local z_user_state
609	z_user_state=$(ps_capture \
610	  "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue") \
611	  || die "Failed to probe local user ${z_user}"
612	z_user_state="${z_user_state//[$'\r\n\t ']/}"
613	if [[ -n "${z_user_state}" ]]; then
614	  priv_ps "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}'" \
615	    || die "Failed to remove local user ${z_user}"
616	fi
617	```
618	
619	### Service running state → conditional start
620	
621	```bash
622	local z_service_state
623	z_service_state=$(ps_capture \
624	  "(Get-Service -Name '${z_svc}').Status") \
625	  || die "Failed to probe service ${z_svc}"
626	if [[ "${z_service_state}" == "Stopped" ]]; then
627	  priv_ps "Start-Service -Name '${z_svc}'" \
628	    || die "Failed to start service ${z_svc}"
629	fi
630	```
631	
632	### Registry key absence → conditional creation
633	
634	```bash
635	local z_present
636	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_regkey}'") \
637	  || die "Failed to probe registry key ${z_regkey}"
638	if [[ "${z_present}" != "True" ]]; then
639	  priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null" \
640	    || die "Failed to create registry key ${z_regkey}"
641	fi
642	```
643	
644	## Wrapper Discipline
645	
646	All three wrappers compose outer `"..."` around `${z_body}` and so apply
647	the WSp-109 escape transform unconditionally.
648	
649	### PowerShell wrapper
650	
651	```bash
652	local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
653	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
654	    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body_escaped}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
655	```
656	
657	Wrapper prelude:
658	- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
659	- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
660	- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (WSp-101)
661	- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes
662	
663	Body authoring follows WSp-102 through WSp-108.
664	
665	### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (w-letter)
666	
667	```bash
668	local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
669	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
670	    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
671	```
672	
673	Body authoring follows the WSs- and WSt- rule families, with WSt-104's
674	w-letter escape table: `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}`;
675	`$(...)` unescaped.
676	
677	### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (c-letter)
678	
679	```bash
680	local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
681	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
682	    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
683	```
684	
685	Body authoring follows the WSs- and WSt- rule families. Per WSt-104's
686	c-letter row, `$name`, `${name}`, and `$(...)` all pass unescaped —
687	cmd.exe alone does no shell substitution.
688	
689	## Deferred
690	
691	The b-letter path (direct ssh to Linux/Mac) bypasses every Windows layer;
692	standard BCG body discipline applies. When adding a non-Windows target,
693	run analog probes substituting plain bash for wsl.exe / cygwin.
694	
695	## Verification Probe Template
696	
697	```
698	# Probe N: <claim>
699	# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>
700	
701	<priv-ssh wrapper> <node> "<failing form>"
702	# stdout: <observed>; exit: <observed>
703	
704	<priv-ssh wrapper> <node> "<repaired form>"
705	# stdout: <observed>; exit: <observed>
706	```
707	
708	Probes are read-only where possible; mutating probes must declare cleanup.
709	
710	## Acronym Registry
711	
712	| Term | Expansion |
713	|------|-----------|
714	| WSG | Windows Scripting Guide (this document) |
715	| OpenSSH-Win32 | The Win32 port of OpenSSH bundled with Windows 10+ |
716	| Curia | The local invoking machine |
717	| Fundus | The remote target machine |
718	
719	## Related Documents
720	
721	- BCG — base bash discipline
722

5	user (auth boundary discovered by Windows OpenSSH), the workload's

10	The third namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design --
11	distributions register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`;
12	admin's distributions are invisible to workload sessions.

15	admin-owned absolute-path authorized_keys.

23	sessions (step 11), each carrying a single cmdlet or native-binary
24	call per WSG WSp-105. The fourth session is `wsl.exe --shutdown`

30	block + admin-owned authorized_keys) is the auth defense if

261	
262	//axhos_step
263	. *Provision workload's WSL (four atomic SSH-as-workload sessions per WSG WSp-105)*:
264	.. {busc_call}
265	*Session 1 (import)*: ssh as
266	{bujb_workload_user}
267	with
268	{burp_workload_key_file}
269	running `wsl.exe --import rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root>
270	«SEED_TARBALL»` -- registers the workload's own `rbtww-main` in
271	workload's `HKCU\\Lxss`; `<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable
272	directory inside the workload's Windows profile. The bare entry at
273	the absolute-path authorized_keys authenticates this session
274	.. {busc_call}
275	*Session 2 (useradd)*: ssh as
276	{bujb_workload_user}
277	running `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
278	--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` -- mints the
279	inner Linux workload user. `useradd` produces a locked-by-default
280	account per `/etc/shadow` convention (shadow entry `!` when `-p`
281	is not supplied); no separate `passwd --lock` session required
282	.. {busc_call}
283	*Session 3 (privkey)*: ssh as
284	{bujb_workload_user}
285	running `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root install -D
286	-m 600 -o ${BUJB_workload_user} -g ${BUJB_workload_user} /dev/stdin
287	/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`, redirecting
288	{burp_workload_key_file}
289	to ssh's stdin -- the key flows through ssh FD 0 -> cmd.exe -> wsl.exe
290	-> Linux child, where `install -D /dev/stdin` sets mode and owner
291	atomically and creates the parent `.ssh/` directory. No plaintext
292	key on either side's disk, no remote temp file, no key material
293	in argv
294	.. {busc_call}
295	*Session 4 (shutdown)*: ssh as
296	{bujb_workload_user}
297	running `wsl.exe --shutdown` -- releases the WSL VM and helper-
298	process hive handles (wslhost.exe, wslservice) before this session's
299	Windows logon unloads. Best-effort defense against demotion
300	accumulation across the four sessions; Layer 1
301	({busn_caparison}-installed absolute-path Match block) is the auth
302	defense if a stray demotion occurs
303	.. {busc_call}
304	delete `«SEED_TARBALL»` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
305	.. {busc_fatal}
306	on any session's failure -- workload state partially provisioned
307	across namespaces; revalidate from a clean garrison
308	
309	//axhos_step
310	. *Lock down workload trust (admin rewrite)*:
311	.. {busc_call}
312	`Set-Content` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` overwriting the absolute-path
313	authorized_keys at
314	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
315	with the locked-down line: directive prefix + `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»`
316	-- single cmdlet per WSG WSp-105; admin-owned file throughout; no
317	in-session rewrite race
318	.. Canonical directive prefix: `BUJB_command_w` in
319	{bujb_jurisdiction}
320	with `${BUJB_workload_user}` substituted at directive-emit time:
321	+
322	[listing]
323	----
324	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
325	----
326	.. {busc_fatal}
327	on rewrite failure -- bare trust still in place; subsequent
328	workload SSH would bypass the locked-down route; revalidate from a
329	clean garrison
330	
331	//axhog_guarantee
332	On success:
333	
334	* Exactly one workload identity named
335	{bujb_workload_user}
336	exists, mirrored across all three namespaces.
337	* Windows local user with SID registered under
338	`HKLM\\...\\ProfileList` (auth boundary).
339	* Workload-owned `rbtww-main` registered in workload's `HKCU\\Lxss`
340	(distribution namespace).
341	* WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context entered
342	by the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`).
343	* Workload authorized_keys at
344	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
345	carries `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` with the locked-down `command=`
346	directive -- admin-owned absolute path; auth decoupled from
347	workload `$HOME` via caparison-installed `Match User` block.
348	* {burp_workload_key_file}
349	planted at `/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` inside the
350	workload's distribution.
351	* `wsl.exe --shutdown` executed inside the workload session before
352	exit; no orphan `NTUSER.DAT` hive handles outlive garrison-w.
353	* Admin posture unchanged -- no `sshd_config` writes, no admin
354	authorized_keys writes.
355	
356	Garrison-w does not self-validate the locked-down round-trip;
357	{bust_invigilate_windows}
358	asserts it as a post-completion fact.
359	
360	//axhoc_completion
361	Exit 0 when step 12's admin-context lockdown rewrite completes
362	after step 11's `wsl.exe --shutdown` and seed cleanup. Non-zero on
363	any step's
364	{busc_fatal}.
365	
366	==== Rationale
367	
368	*Four-session shape with final shutdown (step 11)*::
369	WSG WSp-105 (one cmdlet or native binary per body) forbids the
370	compound session shape (wsl --import + useradd + install-D + wsl
371	--shutdown all on one workload SSH connection). Each cmdlet
372	therefore opens its own session. Across sessions 1-3, helper
373	processes (wslhost.exe, wslservice) spawned by `wsl --import`
374	outlive their parent session and lock the NTUSER.DAT hive of the
375	workload's logon, exposing the demotion path (User Profile Service
376	relocating the canonical profile to `bujuw_user.<host>.NNN` on the
377	next logon). Session 4 is `wsl.exe --shutdown` as its only act,
378	releasing the WSL VM and the helper-process hive handles before
379	that session's logon unloads -- best-effort cleanup that limits but
380	does not eliminate the demotion risk across sessions 1-3. Layer 1
381	({busn_caparison}-installed absolute-path Match block + admin-owned
382	authorized_keys) is the auth defense if demotion still occurs:
383	sshd reads as SYSTEM from the absolute path and never resolves the
384	workload's `$HOME`. The lockdown rewrite (step 12) runs in admin
385	context after session 4 exits -- no in-session rewrite race.
386	
387	*Two LIKE patterns in Phase 2*::
388	The query matches `LocalPath LIKE
389	'C:\Users\${BUJB_workload_user}%'` AND `LocalPath LIKE
390	'C:\Users\${BUJB_workload_user}.%'`. The first catches the
391	canonical path; the second catches numbered fallback dirs from
392	prior demotions (`bujuw_user.<host>.NNN`). One pattern alone leaks
393	state.
394	
395	*Per-row Remove-CimInstance in Phase 2*::
396	WSG WSp-105 mandates one cmdlet per SSH body. The probe emits
397	rows; bash iterates and issues one Remove-CimInstance per row with
398	a LocalPath-specific `-Query`. A piped pattern (probe |
399	remove-CimInstance) would violate WSp-105 and is forbidden.
400	
401	*Phase 5 is admin's rbtww-main, not workload's*::
402	Workload's distribution lives in workload's own `HKCU\Lxss`,
403	populated in step 11. Phase 5 cleans the workload's Linux user
404	vestige inside *admin's* `rbtww-main` -- vestige from prior
405	garrison shapes when workload shared admin's distribution. The
406	phase is retained because residual entries leak shell history and
407	HKCU pollution across garrisons.
408	
409	==== References
410	
411	* link:https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/administration/openssh/openssh_server_configuration[Microsoft Learn -- OpenSSH Server Configuration for Windows] (AuthorizedKeysFile, Match blocks)
412	* link:https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1383[Win32-OpenSSH #1383] -- local-user SID registration under ProfileList required for OpenSSH logon
413	* link:https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/user-profiles-and-logon/profile-loading-fails[Microsoft Learn -- Profile loading fails]
414	* link:https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/setup-upgrade-and-drivers/scripts-clean-profile-folder-prevent-temp-profile[Microsoft Learn -- Scripts to clean profile folder and prevent TEMP user profile creation]
415	* link:https://helgeklein.com/free-tools/delprof2-user-profile-deletion-tool/[helgeklein.com -- Delprof2] -- documented authority for safe profile teardown sequencing
416	* WSG WSp-105 -- one cmdlet or one native binary per SSH body (no compound PowerShell bodies)
417

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_bash}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
5	for Linux and macOS nodes.
6	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
7	{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
8	`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
9	single greppable destroy verb. On Linux the helper runs `userdel -r`
10	and purges `/home/<user>`; on macOS it runs `sysadminctl -deleteUser`
11	and purges `/Users/<user>`. Each step is idempotent on absent state.
12	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
13	admin authorized_keys.
14	
15	//axhopt_typed_parameter
16	{burp_investiture}
17	
18	//axhoq_precondition
19	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
20	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
21	
22	//axhoq_precondition
23	sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
24	by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac admin
25	trust is established via {busn_caparison}, which wraps the
26	operator-managed `ssh-copy-id` flow rather than performing autonomous
27	SSH-trust hardening (only Windows OpenSSH has the autonomous
28	phase-1/phase-2 ceremony).
29	
30	//axhoq_precondition
31	{bust_invigilate_macos} or {bust_invigilate_linux} passes for this
32	{burp_investiture} (selected by the BURN-targeted OS): admin SSH
33	key trust is in place and host posture (Remote Login + pmset +
34	Tailscale launchd on macOS; sshd + sleep-target mask + tailscaled on
35	Linux) is correct. Garrison runs invigilate as its first action so a
36	missing or stale admin posture surfaces with a fact-named diagnostic
37	rather than as an opaque downstream failure.
38	
39	//axhoq_precondition
40	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
41	station.
42	
43	//axhos_step
44	. *Assert admin posture*: invoke `{bust_invigilate_macos}` or
45	`{bust_invigilate_linux}` for this {burp_investiture} (selected by
46	the BURN-targeted OS). Failure surfaces a fact-named diagnostic
47	({bust_handbook_macos}/{bust_handbook_linux} pointer for
48	operator-managed facts;
49	{bust_caparison_macos}/{bust_caparison_linux} pointer for
50	caparison-managed facts) and aborts garrison before any destructive
51	workload work.
52	
53	//axhos_step
54	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
55	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
56	missing or stale admin trust.
57	
58	//axhos_step
59	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`. On Linux
60	the helper runs `sudo -n userdel -r` and `sudo -n rm -rf /home/<user>`;
61	on macOS it runs `sudo -n sysadminctl -deleteUser` and
62	`sudo -n rm -rf /Users/<user>`. Idempotent on absent state.
63	
64	//axhos_step
65	. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
66	ssh-only account.
67	
68	//axhos_step
69	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
70	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
71	the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
72	mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
73	+
74	[listing]
75	----
76	command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
77	----
78	
79	//axhos_step
80	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
81	account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
82	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
83	
84	//axhos_step
85	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
86	exit 0.
87	
88	//axhog_guarantee
89	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
90	{bujb_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
91	outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.
92	
93	//axhoc_completion
94	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
95	Non-zero on invigilate assertion failure, admin SSH failure,
96	account-management failure, key-write failure, or workload validation
97	failure.
